blocks.content: The room she'd grown up in knew all her versions.
The one who climbed the bookshelf at seven and couldn't get down.
The one who stayed up all night reading because she couldn't bear to leave an ending unread.
The one who learned, at sixteen, that bodies could betray you without warning.
That last one she didn't think about directly.
Not often.

It lived in the edges of things instead. The small rituals at the end of every day. The appointments she kept quietly, rescheduled quietly, recovered from quietly. The way she'd learned to calibrate—push too hard on certain days, and the next two would feel like walking through water.
Most people didn't notice.
She'd become very good at making sure they didn't.
It wasn't dishonesty, exactly.
It was more like—
Not letting the answer to 'how are you' become a weight someone else had to carry.
She looked at Harold.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: He's an artist.
She said it softly, to the room, to the bear, to no one in particular.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: He paints things people miss.
Harold offered nothing.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: He gave you to me.
She pressed the bear slightly closer.
Her chest felt tight in a way that had nothing to do with her condition.
Or—
Maybe it had everything to do with it.
She thought about tomorrow.
He'd said it. She'd said it. They'd said it to each other like a promise, standing on a sidewalk corner as the day went gold around them.
She knew the careful thing.
The careful thing was to slow down. Take inventory. Think about what it meant to let someone in when there were parts of yourself you couldn't explain yet—not to them, not even fully to yourself.
She knew what careful looked like.
Maya's texts. Lena's quiet, steady watching. The medication on her nightstand. The calendar appointments marked in a color she didn't show anyone.
She knew.
And then she thought about the way Ethan had looked at Gerald before handing him over.
Like it was the most natural gift.
Like he wasn't even calculating the weight of it.
She smiled.
Careful.
And then, quietly, honestly—
Not tonight.
She closed her eyes.
Outside, the street held its breath.
And somewhere across the city, she was almost certain, Ethan was lying in the same dark, thinking the same word.
Tomorrow.
Not as a plan.
As a choice.
